Agent Architecture

How messages flow through the system, from user input to AI response

System Overview

Messages come in from users, the AI thinks about them, takes actions, saves what it learns, and keeps everything safe. Click any box for more detail.

Detailed Data Flow

Step-by-Step: A Message From Start to Finish

A user sends "What was our Q2 marketing budget?" via Telegram. Follow the 8 steps below, plus the session management interactions (marked S), security checks, and monitoring events that happen behind the scenes.

How does the AI know what tools are available?

At startup, the system registers all enabled tools (shell, file read/write, memory, browser, delegate, etc.) and builds a tool registry. Before each AI request (Step 5), the system converts every registered tool into a schema — its name, description, and accepted parameters — and sends these alongside the prompt. The AI model sees this menu and decides which tool to call (or none). The system never hard-codes which tool runs; the AI picks from whatever is registered.

How does monitoring get triggered? (The grey boxes on the right)

Monitoring doesn't run on a schedule or poll for data. Instead, each component has a single line of code that pushes a notification at the exact moment something happens. Think of it like an employee shouting "done!" after finishing a task.

Here's exactly what triggers each monitoring event in this flow:

When
What happens in the code
Event fired
Message arrives
The channel adapter receives a message and immediately calls observer.record_event()
ChannelMessage
AI call starts
Right before sending the prompt to the AI, the loop fires an event
LlmRequest
AI responds
The moment the AI response is received, another event fires with token counts and latency
LlmResponse
Tool runs
Before running a tool: ToolCallStart. After it finishes: ToolCall with how long it took and whether it succeeded
ToolCallStart, ToolCall
Turn ends
When the agent finishes its full reply, a summary event records total tokens, cost, and duration
TurnComplete

The key idea: the code that does the work is the same code that fires the event. There's no separate monitoring process watching from the outside. At startup, the system creates an Observer object and shares it with every component. Each component calls observer.record_event() at the right moment. The Observer then writes to whatever backends you configured (log files, Prometheus metrics, OpenTelemetry traces, or all of them).

Interactive Demo: Walk Through a Message

Click Next Step to follow a real message through the system. Watch each component light up in the sequence diagram as data flows through each step.

Click Next to begin
Ready to start

Click Next Step to send the message "What was our Q2 marketing budget?" from Telegram and watch it flow through all 9 participants.

Core Components

Channels & Gateway

The system connects to 29 messaging platforms at the same time. Each platform adapter can send and receive messages, show typing indicators, stream partial responses as the AI thinks, add reactions, and pin important messages.

TelegramDiscordSlackWhatsAppMatrixiMessageEmailSignalMattermostLarkTwitterRedditBlueskyNostrIRCQQWeComDingTalkVoiceCallVoiceWakeCLIWebhookClawdTalkGmailPushWATIMochatLinqNextcloudTalk

Web API (Gateway) — A built-in web server that lets external apps talk to the agent over HTTP. Supports standard web requests, real-time connections (WebSockets), and live event streams. Protected by authentication (one-time pairing codes), rate limiting, and encryption.

Public Access (Tunnel) — Makes the agent reachable from the internet, even if it's running on your local computer. Seven tunnel services are supported so platforms like Telegram or Slack can send webhook notifications.

CloudflareTailscaleNgrokPinggyOpenVPNCustomNone

Thinking Loop

This is the brain of the system. When a message arrives, it goes through a repeating cycle:

  1. Build context — Combines your message with personality, relevant memories, skill instructions, and language settings into one package for the AI.
  2. Ask the AI — Sends everything to the chosen AI model. Partial updates are streamed back so you see the reply forming in real time.
  3. Take action — If the AI needs to do something (search the web, save a file, etc.), the system runs a safety check, optionally asks for approval, then executes.
  4. Repeat — If the AI needs more steps, the cycle continues. Once it has a final answer, it's sent back to you.

Supporting features: message classification, memory retrieval, budget tracking, reasoning traces, and conversation history management.

AI Models

The system can use 17 different AI services. You choose which one in settings and can switch mid-conversation. Each can answer questions, stream responses word-by-word, and understand images/media (if supported).

OpenAIAnthropicGoogle GeminiOllama (local)OpenRouterAzure OpenAIAWS BedrockCustom / Compatible API15+ providers supported

Auto-retry: If one service is down, the system tries the next. Smart routing: rules like "use Provider A for coding, Provider B for writing".

Action Parser

When the AI decides to take action, the Action Parser reads the AI's output and figures out exactly what to run:

  • Built-in support — Modern AI models directly specify actions in structured JSON format.
  • Text parsing — For simpler models, the system reads specially formatted text (XML tags, markdown blocks, GLM-style, etc.) in the response.

Actions (Tools)

Over 30 built-in actions the AI can take on your behalf. Each has a name, description (so the AI knows when to use it), and defined inputs/outputs.

Run commandsRead/write filesFind filesSearch contentGit operationsBrowse websitesWeb requestsWeb searchFetch pagesCalculatorWeatherDraw on canvasRemember/recallSchedule tasksSwitch AI modelForward to agentSend notificationsDelegate workParallel tasksMulti-step workflowsSigned actionsAsk questionsEscalate to humanReact to messagesExternal plugins

Safety guardrails: actions limited to your workspace, rate-limited to prevent runaway loops, and shell commands can optionally run in an isolated sandbox.

Hardware

The agent can connect to physical hardware — microcontrollers, sensors, and single-board computers. Once connected, capabilities automatically become available as actions.

STM32 boardsRaspberry PiArduinoESP32HTTP-bridged devicesSerial devices

Skills & App Connectors

Skills are add-on capability packs — like apps for your phone. Each teaches the agent something new. Skills can be browsed, installed, safety-checked, and tested.

App Connectors link the agent to third-party services (Notion, Jira, Microsoft 365, LinkedIn, etc.). The agent can read and write to these services on your behalf.

Plug & Play: How Components Are Swapped

The system uses a design pattern called factories to let you swap out major components without changing any other code. You pick what you want in the config file, and the system builds the right piece at startup. The rest of the code never knows or cares which specific piece is running behind the scenes.

The core idea

Every major component (AI provider, memory, channel, monitoring) follows the same contract — a shared interface that says "here's what you must be able to do." The factory reads your config, picks the matching implementation, wraps it up, and hands it to the rest of the system. From that point on, every other component just talks to the interface, not the specific implementation.

How it works, step by step

1
You write a config line — for example, provider = "anthropic" or memory_backend = "sqlite"
2
At startup, a factory function reads that string and uses a match statement to pick the right implementation. Think of it like a dispatcher: "anthropic" → build Anthropic provider, "ollama" → build Ollama provider, etc.
3
The factory wraps it in a trait object — a generic box that hides which specific implementation is inside. The box only exposes the shared interface (e.g., "send a prompt, get a response").
4
The rest of the system receives the box and uses it without knowing what's inside. The Thinking Loop calls provider.send() — it works identically whether that's Anthropic, Ollama, OpenAI, or a custom endpoint.

Every major component uses this pattern

Component Config value What the factory builds Shared interface
AI Provider "anthropic", "ollama", "openai", "openrouter", "custom:https://..." The specific API client for that service A generic Provider interface
Memory "sqlite", "markdown", "vector-db", "none" The storage engine for agent memory A generic Memory interface
Channels "telegram", "discord", "slack", "signal", ... The messaging platform adapter A generic Channel interface
Monitoring "log", "prometheus", "otel", "none" The monitoring backend A generic Observer interface
Embeddings "openai", "openrouter", "custom:https://..." The vector embedding service for semantic search A generic EmbeddingProvider interface

Concrete example: switching AI providers

Suppose you're using Anthropic and want to switch to a local Ollama model. Here's what changes:

# Before (config.toml)
provider = "anthropic"
model = "gpt-4o"
api_key = "sk-ant-..."
# After (config.toml)
provider = "ollama"
model = "llama3"
# no api_key needed

That's it — two lines changed. No code changes. The factory reads "ollama", builds the Ollama adapter, wraps it behind the generic Provider interface, and hands it to the Thinking Loop. The loop doesn't know or care that it's now talking to a local model instead of a cloud API.

What about unknown values?

Every factory has a fallback. If you type memory_backend = "foobar", the memory factory logs a warning and falls back to markdown. If you type provider = "foobar", the provider factory returns a clear error telling you which providers are supported. No crashes, no silent failures.

Concrete example: switching memory backends

Same pattern, different component. Switch from flat files to a database:

# Before
memory_backend = "markdown"
# stores memories as .md files
# After
memory_backend = "sqlite"
# stores memories in a SQLite database
# with vector search via embeddings

The agent's behavior stays identical — memory.store(), memory.search(), memory.list() all work the same way. Only the underlying storage changes.

Tools: a different pattern

Tools don't use a config-driven factory. Instead, they use a registry — at startup, the system builds a list of all enabled tools and hands them to the AI as a menu. Each tool implements the Tool trait (name, description, parameters, execute). The AI picks which to call based on the user's request. New tools are added by implementing the trait and pushing them into the registry — no existing code needs to change.

Why this matters

The factory pattern is what makes the "swap anything" promise work. Want to move from cloud AI to local? Change one line. Want to switch from file-based memory to a vector database? Change one line. Want to add Prometheus monitoring on top of log files? Add one config entry. The system assembles itself from your config at startup, and every component only talks through shared interfaces — never directly to a specific implementation.

The Message Bus: MPSC Channels

Once all components are built by the factories, they need a way to talk to each other. The system uses a pattern called MPSC (Multiple Producers, Single Consumer) — think of it like a mailbox where many people can drop letters in, but one person reads them all.

The mailbox analogy

Imagine you run a help desk. You have one inbox (the receiver), and multiple support channels — phone, email, walk-ins — each dropping tickets into the same inbox (the senders). You pick up tickets one at a time and handle them. That's exactly how MPSC works.

Here's how the main message flow uses this pattern:

1
One shared mailbox is created — mpsc::channel(100) creates a sender/receiver pair with room for 100 messages. The receiver (rx) stays in the main dispatch loop. Each channel gets a clone of the sender (tx).
2
Each channel listens on its own platform — Telegram polls its API, Discord holds a WebSocket, Slack listens for webhooks. When a message arrives, the channel wraps it into a standard message struct and drops it into the shared mailbox via its sender clone.
3
The dispatch loop reads from the mailbox — a single while let Some(msg) = rx.recv().await loop pulls messages one by one. It doesn't know or care which platform sent the message — it just processes the standard struct.
4
Each message is spawned as a worker task — the loop acquires a semaphore permit (to limit concurrent processing), then spawns an async task to handle the message: load history, call the AI, run tools, send the reply. Multiple messages from different senders are processed in parallel.

This is used in multiple places, not just the main message flow:

MPSC Channel Senders (many) Receiver (one) Purpose
Main message bus Telegram, Discord, Slack, Signal, etc. Dispatch loop Routes incoming user messages to the AI
Draft streaming Thinking Loop (as AI generates text) Draft updater task Pushes partial responses to the chat in real time so users see typing progress
Tool notifications Observer wrapper (on each tool event) Notification sender task Posts live "running tool X..." messages to the chat thread

The Event Loop: How Everything Runs Concurrently

The agent is built on an async runtime. Instead of threads blocking and waiting, tasks yield when they're waiting for something (an API response, a timer, a message) and let other tasks run. The system uses two key patterns to manage this:

Spawn (background tasks)

Launches a task that runs independently in the background. Used for:

  • Each channel listener (Telegram poller, Discord WebSocket, etc.)
  • Each message worker (one per incoming message)
  • The draft updater that streams partial AI responses
  • The gateway HTTP server
  • The cron scheduler
Select / Race (first wins)

Waits for whichever happens first among multiple async operations. Used for:

  • Channel listeners: wait for a message or a health tick — whichever comes first
  • AI calls: wait for the response or a cancellation signal or a timeout
  • Typing indicators: send a refresh or stop when the reply is ready
  • Gateway: accept a connection or handle a shutdown signal

Putting it all together: the monitoring event loop

Now you can see how monitoring actually flows through all of this:

Startup — The factory creates an Observer (e.g., a Prometheus observer). It's wrapped in a thread-safe reference so it can be shared safely across async tasks.
▼
Observer is passed everywhere — It goes into the shared runtime context which is available to every spawned message worker. It also goes into the gateway, the cron scheduler, and the heartbeat timer.
▼
Channel listener receives a message — A Telegram message arrives. The listener wraps it into a standard message object and drops it into the MPSC mailbox. No monitoring event here — the listener's job is just delivery.
▼
Dispatch loop picks up the message — The while let Some(msg) = rx.recv() loop receives it, acquires a concurrency permit, and spawns a worker task.
▼
Inside the worker task, monitoring fires inline — The worker wraps the shared observer in a notification wrapper that both records the event and sends a live update to the chat via a second MPSC channel. At each step — before the AI call, after the AI responds, before a tool runs, after it finishes — the code calls observer.record_event() synchronously. The observer never blocks; it writes to an in-memory counter (Prometheus) or appends to a buffer (log/OTel) and returns immediately. The worker continues without waiting.
▼
Meanwhile, race conditions keep everything alive — While the worker processes the message, the channel listener races listening for the next message against health ticks — whichever happens first wins. In the AI call, another race runs the LLM response against a timeout and a cancellation signal. If the user sends /stop, the cancellation fires and the race picks it up immediately — the AI call is aborted and a "turn complete" monitoring event fires.
No polling, no background watcher

The entire monitoring flow is synchronous and inline. There's no separate thread or timer that periodically asks "what happened?" Instead, the same async task that does the work is the one that fires the event. The Observer is called on the hot path, so it must never block — it writes to memory and returns. The only "pull" in the system is Prometheus scraping GET /metrics on a timer, but even that just reads in-memory counters that were pushed by the observer.

Memory & Context

How the Agent Remembers Things

The agent can remember information across conversations. It saves facts, recalls them by searching for related topics (not just exact keywords), lists everything it knows, and forgets when asked. Six storage options:

SQLite (database)Markdown filesLucid (advanced SQLite)Qdrant (meaning-based search)None (no memory)Namespaced (isolated per topic)

Additional features: response caching, multi-step retrieval, knowledge graphs, and meaning-based search (finds relevant memories even with different wording).

Session Management

Every conversation has a session — the system keeps track of what was said so the AI has context for follow-up messages.

What defines a "session"?

Each conversation is identified by a unique key:

{channel} + {chat_id} + {thread} + {sender}

Different chats have separate histories. Different threads are separate. Different users get their own context.

Where are sessions stored?

In memory: Fast LRU cache holding up to 1,000 sessions, each keeping the last 50 messages. Oldest evicted when full.

On disk: Each session saved as a file in the sessions directory. Messages appended line-by-line. On startup, the most recent sessions are reloaded.

Context Window Management

AI models have a limited "memory" per call (the context window, typically 32K–128K tokens). The system manages this carefully:

1. Token Budget

Before each AI call, estimate token count (~4 chars = 1 token). Default budget: 32,000 tokens.

2. Smart Trimming

Over budget? Old action results compressed first (keep first 2/3 + last 1/3). Then older messages dropped, keeping 4 most recent intact.

3. Context Compression

For long conversations, the AI summarizes older history into a condensed recap, keeping the thread without full verbatim text.

4. Emergency Recovery

If the AI rejects a request (too long), the system auto-trims old action details + drops oldest 1/3 of messages, then retries.

5. Tool Context Cleanup

Only the last 2 turns keep full action details. Older turns have action context stripped to save space.

Identity & Personality

Before the AI sees your message (Step 4 in the flow), the system assembles a system prompt that tells the AI who it is, how to behave, what it knows, and what tools it has. Built from files in your workspace:

Personality File → User Profile → Identity Config → Agent Instructions → Tool Definitions

The Personality File contains core personality, values, and behavioral rules. The User Profile holds info about you (preferences, context). These are read on every message, trimmed to a configurable max length, and combined into the system prompt.

File not found?

If a personality file is missing, the AI is told that it's unavailable. If empty, it's silently skipped. You can use markdown or structured JSON formats for identity configuration.

What the AI Receives (Assembly Order)

Safety Rules → Personality + User Profile → Available Tools → Skills → Recalled Memories → Instructions + Tool Defs

This entire prompt is the first message every time the AI is called. Your conversation history and new question follow after it.

Runtime, Settings & Document Search

Runtime — Controls where the agent runs commands: locally on your machine or inside a Docker container for added safety.

Settings — One configuration file controls everything: which AI to use, platforms to connect, security rules, memory storage, scheduled tasks, monitoring, and integrations. Workspaces keep projects isolated.

Document Search — The agent can search through reference documents (PDFs, datasheets) to find specific information. Large documents are broken into searchable chunks and the most relevant sections are retrieved.

Security

Autonomy Levels

You decide how much freedom the agent has. Three modes:

Read-Only

Can only look, never change anything.

Supervised

Asks you before doing anything risky.

Full

Acts independently within configured bounds.

The system classifies how risky each action is and limits how often any single user can trigger actions.

Security Controls Every Tool

Every single tool call (shell, file, memory, delegate, etc.) passes through multiple security layers before it runs. This applies equally to the parent agent and every sub-agent:

Checks before ANY tool runs:

  1. Autonomy Level — ReadOnly blocks all actions. Supervised requires approval for risky ones.
  2. Rate Limiter — Max actions per hour per sender. Prevents runaway loops.
  3. Path Guard — Blocks access to forbidden files (e.g. /etc/passwd, secrets).
  4. Command Allowlist — Shell commands must be on the allowed list. Unknown = rejected.
  5. Risk Assessment — Commands scored as Low / Medium / High risk. High-risk can be blocked.

Extra checks for delegation:

  1. Delegate tool itself is security-checked — blocked in ReadOnly, rate-limited like any other tool.
  2. Sub-agent tools are filtered — only tools in allowed_tools are available.
  3. Depth limit — max_depth prevents sub→sub→sub chains.
  4. No self-delegation — the delegate tool is always excluded from sub-agent tool lists.
  5. Security wrappers inherited — rate limits, path guards, command checks still apply.
Example: What happens when the AI tries to run curl

1. AI says: shell(command="curl http://example.com")
2. RateLimitedTool wrapper checks rate budget → OK
3. PathGuardedTool wrapper checks path → OK (no file path)
4. Shell tool calls security.validate_command_execution("curl ...")
5. Security checks allowed_commands list from config
6. BLOCKED — curl is not in the default allowed list
7. Error returned to AI: "Command not allowed by security policy"

Default allowed: git, npm, cargo, ls, cat, grep, find, echo, pwd, wc, head, tail, date, df, du, uname, uptime, python, python3, pip, node
Even if you add curl, it's flagged as high-risk and may need explicit approval in Supervised mode.

Sandbox (Isolation)

When the agent runs commands, it can do so inside an isolated "sandbox" that prevents touching anything outside the workspace:

DockerFirejailBubblewrapLandlock (Linux)Seatbelt (macOS)

Human Approval

For high-risk actions, the agent pauses and asks for your confirmation. You can approve once, deny, or say "always allow this" for that action.

Trust Score & Action Proof

The system tracks performance over time. If mistakes happen, trust score drops and autonomy may be reduced. For critical actions, cryptographic signing creates a tamper-proof record of exactly what was authorized and by whom.

Additional protections: prompt injection detection, access control policies, hardware-key authentication, emergency stop, secrets management, and audit logging.

Security Configuration

level = "supervised"
# read_only | supervised | full

allowed_commands = [
  "git", "cargo", "ls", "cat", ...
]

forbidden_paths = [
  "/etc", "~/.ssh", "/root"
]

max_actions_per_hour = 20
block_high_risk_commands = true

Sub-Agent Delegation

What is a sub-agent?

A sub-agent is a named specialist you pre-define in the config file. Each one has its own AI model, personality, and limited set of tools. Think of it like hiring a freelancer: you describe who they are and what they can do, but they only start working when the main AI decides it needs help.

Sub-agents do NOT run on their own. They only exist when the parent AI explicitly calls the delegate tool — just like any other tool (shell, file, memory). No separate process, no separate server.

Sub-agents do NOT receive the parent's personality or user profile files. Those belong to the parent only. Each sub-agent gets its own identity from the system_prompt field in its config. They also don't see the parent's session history — they start with a clean slate each time.

Step 1: Define sub-agents in config

[agents.researcher]
provider = "anthropic"
model = "gpt-4o"
system_prompt = "You are a research specialist"
agentic = true
allowed_tools = ["web_search", "read_file"]
max_depth = 3
max_iterations = 10

provider + model — which AI to use (can differ from parent)

system_prompt — the sub-agent's personality (replaces parent's personality files)

agentic = true — can use tools in a loop (not just answer once)

allowed_tools — only these tools are available (filtered from parent's tools)

max_depth — levels of sub→sub delegation allowed

Step 2: The parent AI decides to delegate

When you define sub-agents in config, the system automatically adds a delegate tool to the parent AI's tool list. The parent AI sees it like any other tool and chooses to use it when a task fits a specialist.

What the parent AI returns:
tool_call: delegate(agent="researcher", prompt="Find competitor pricing for Q2")

→ The Thinking Loop sees this tool call and runs it, just like shell() or memory_save(). The AI decides; the system executes.

Step 3: What happens inside the delegate tool

Execution Modes

Simple mode

agentic = false
One AI call → get answer → done. No tools, no loop.

Agentic mode

agentic = true
AI calls tools → gets results → repeats. Full tool loop, same as parent.

Background mode

background: true
Returns task_id immediately. Check later with check_result(task_id).

Swarm mode (multiple agents at once)

Sequential: run agents one after another  |  Parallel: run all at the same time, collect results  |  Router: AI picks the best specialist for the task

Multi-Agent Nodes

Nodes are a separate feature — they connect multiple independent agent instances across machines via the gateway for distributed work. Unlike sub-agents (which run inside the same process), nodes are separate deployments that coordinate over the network.

Operations

Monitoring & Budget

The system keeps detailed records of everything: AI calls, actions taken, messages sent, cache usage, scheduled task runs, and errors.

How monitoring gets triggered

There is no separate background process watching the system. Instead, the code that does the actual work is the same code that fires the monitoring event. Think of it like a kitchen where each cook shouts out what they just did:

  1. At startup, the system creates one Observer object and hands it to every component (the Thinking Loop, tool executor, channels, gateway, heartbeat timer). Every component now holds a reference to the same Observer.
  2. Each component fires events at specific moments:
    • Thinking Loop shouts: "starting!", "asking the AI!", "got an answer!", "done with this turn"
    • Tool executor shouts: "about to run a tool!", "tool finished, took 230ms, success"
    • Channel shouts: "message came in!" or "message sent!"
  3. The Observer receives these shouts and writes them to whatever backends you configured:
Log files
Writes structured text lines to a file. Good for debugging. Human-readable.
Prometheus
Updates in-memory counters (requests, tokens, errors). Prometheus scrapes GET /metrics periodically.
OpenTelemetry
Pushes traces to a collector. Each message flow becomes a traceable span with timing.
Runtime Trace
Appends JSON events to a local file. Useful for replay and analysis.
Multiple at once
A composite observer fans out every event to all configured backends simultaneously.

The only "pull" element: Prometheus periodically fetches /metrics from the agent. But the agent itself always pushes — it never waits to be asked.

Budget control tracks spending on AI calls per conversation and per session. Set a spending limit and the agent stops when reached. Health Check runs diagnostics across every subsystem.

Scheduled Tasks

All automation features feed work back through the same thinking cycle, so the agent handles scheduled tasks exactly like user messages:

Schedules — Run tasks on a timer: every hour, daily at 9am, once next Tuesday, or after a delay.

Playbooks — Multi-step procedures like a recipe. Example: "When a new deploy happens, run tests, check logs, post a summary to Slack."

Auto-Rules — "When X happens, do Y" rules. Example: "When someone mentions 'urgent', send a notification."

Recurring Jobs — Background tasks that run periodically with their own context. Each run is logged.

Event Hooks — Custom logic at key moments: server start, session open, before/after AI responds, before/after action runs.

Heartbeat — The agent can run as a background service with periodic check-ins and start/stop/restart management.

Configuration

AI Provider Setup
provider = "openai"
model = "gpt-4"
temperature = 0.7

# If OpenAI goes down:
fallbacks = ["anthropic", "ollama"]

# Smart routing:
route "fast" → Groq
route "code" → Provider B

17 providers built in. Auto-retry and fallback if one is down.

Monitoring Setup
backend = "log"
# log | verbose | prometheus | otel

# For enterprise dashboards:
backend = "otel"
endpoint = "http://..."
service = "my-agent"

Tracks: AI calls, actions, costs, errors, latency. Health check diagnoses all subsystems.

Security Rules
level = "supervised"

allowed_commands = [
  "git", "cargo", "ls", ...
]

forbidden_paths = [
  "/etc", "~/.ssh"
]

max_actions_per_hour = 20

Extensible Design — Everything is Pluggable

Every major component follows the same plug-and-play pattern: define what it does, then swap in any implementation.

AI Models
17
OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama, Bedrock, Groq, Azure, OpenRouter...
Messaging Apps
29
Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Email, Matrix, Signal, iMessage...
Actions
30+
Shell, files, git, browser, web search, calculator, memory, cron...
Memory Stores
6
SQLite, Markdown, Qdrant, Lucid, Namespaced, None
Want to add a new AI provider?

1. Implement 3 methods: chat, stream, and capabilities
2. Register it in the provider list (one line)
3. Use it: provider = "my-custom-ai"

Same pattern for everything else:
+ New channel + New action + New memory store + New monitor + New hardware

Each component defines an interface. Implement it, register it, set in config. No core changes needed.
